items.passage: Hurricanes are powerful storms that develop over warm tropical oceans during the late summer. They begin when moist air rises quickly from the sea and forms towering clouds above the water. As this warm air climbs higher, cooler air rushes in below and starts to spin. The rotation grows stronger, and a calm center known as the eye appears in the middle. Coastal towns often suffer serious damage when these enormous storms finally reach the land.

Young children learn to speak their first language with remarkable speed and almost no formal teaching. By listening to the people around them, they slowly discover the sounds and patterns that carry meaning. Most toddlers begin to combine simple words into short phrases before their second birthday arrives. Their growing vocabulary allows them to express needs, ask questions, and describe the world in richer detail. Researchers believe that the human brain is specially prepared for this early language task.

The division of labor is a simple idea that has shaped human work for thousands of years. When each person concentrates on a single task, the whole group produces far more goods than before. A worker who repeats one job every day becomes faster, more skilled, and less likely to make mistakes. Factories in the past used this principle to turn out clothing, tools, and machines at surprising speed. Modern economies still depend heavily on such careful specialization among countless different workers.

String instruments such as the violin and guitar create music through the steady vibration of tight strings. When a player plucks or bows a string, it moves rapidly back and forth many times each second. These vibrations pass into the wooden body, which amplifies the sound and fills the room with rich tones. Shorter and thinner strings vibrate quickly and produce the high notes that listeners find bright. Skilled musicians control pitch by pressing the strings firmly against the neck.

Ancient Roman builders developed a remarkably strong concrete that still survives after two thousand years. They mixed volcanic ash with lime and water, creating a material that hardened even beneath the sea. Using this concrete, engineers raised tall aqueducts that carried fresh water across valleys and into distant cities. The graceful arches spread heavy weight evenly and allowed the structures to stand without modern steel supports. Many of these impressive works remain standing today as monuments to careful design.

Vaccines protect people from dangerous diseases by teaching the body to recognize harmful germs in advance. Each vaccine contains a weakened or lifeless form of a virus that cannot cause real illness. When the immune system meets this harmless copy, it learns to build tiny defenders called antibodies. Later, if the true germ ever enters the body, these antibodies attack it quickly before sickness can spread. Because of this clever training, many deadly diseases have become rare around the world.
